But I personally, I take a lot of satisfaction. Of course, as you see, I saved them. In fact, I have a non-laminated copy to use in scanning and, and I have a laminated copy that I just laminated in case something ever happened to them. So it was a big deal, believe me. So, so that's, that's my story. And, and so if you like, let's get into the, now the meat of the matter. If you like simple marketing strategies that are easy and inexpensive to implement and you're currently or plan to be involved in a highly competitive niche, then pay, pay close attention because what I'm about to share with you can literally, can literally make you a million dollars or more. The first strategy, the first system I'm going to talk about, I've called Cardboard Hero. And let me give you a little background on what Cardboard Hero is. And frankly, this came about quite by accident. I have been helping some clients who I started working with attorneys. I started my company 15 years ago, but in the last five to seven years, I've had a lot of non-attorneys actually asked me to help them with their marketing and that's why I created Juggular Marketing. And so I had, I had a client who I was working with and we did a direct mail piece and he had a profile about himself and it was nice and glossy and it looked like a photo. So I figured let's put, you know, a piece of stiff cardboard in there so that no one, you know, breaks the photo, tears the photo, folds, folds the piece and I did. And he sent the mail piece out and then he called me and he said, you know, the strangest thing happened. I said, what? He said, I have all these people, almost everyone who responded told me that the mail carrier actually brought this piece directly to their door and hand delivered it to me. I said, really? And he said, yeah. And he said, I guess because it said, do not bend. And we had the cardboard piece in there. It came right to my door. He said, you know what? The crazy thing is, if that's not crazy, you know what the really strange thing was? I said, what? He said, they left all the other mail in the box. This was the only piece that they actually hand delivered. And ladies and gentlemen, at that point, I knew I was potentially onto something very, very, very powerful. And it has been. Let me give you some of the details here. Now, the cardboard hero method costs less than $2.50 per piece. And that includes everything. Includes the mail. It includes the insert, includes the envelope. It includes the letter and includes everything else that you need. About $2.50, a little less than $2.50. But it creates a FedEx effect. And that effect is someone coming to your door and personally delivering the piece with your name on it. And in many, many times leaving all the other mail. Now, I wasn't able to conduct an actual test to give me exact numbers of how many times it was hand delivered, but based on anecdotal evidence, based on the questions that we asked. It was about 70% of the pieces that were mailed were actually hand delivered. Now, not all of the mail carriers left the other mail in the box, but that's an incredible, incredible percent of letters hand delivered. And currently, if you want to send a FedEx, you're going to spend about $33, a little over $33. Now, before I get into the nitty gritty of this, let me ask you a question. Are you doing something right now? Are you involved in a business or about to be involved in a business where you actually compete with other people sending to the same audience? That's one thing. Or are you in a business or trying to get into a business to do direct mail where you want to give your piece that FedEx effect without paying FedEx prices? Ladies and gentlemen, this is an incredible little secret that I've never shared. And if you're able to really master this, like the lawyers who I have lawyers who are literally mailing with other lawyers and the recipient is receiving 20 and 30 direct mail pieces, like for example, someone gets injured. The person who's injured will receive 20 or 30 direct mail pieces from lawyers who want to represent that client. And so the cardboard hero method lets you absolutely blow everyone else out of the water because you're the only piece that they get personally delivered. Now, the centerpiece of the cardboard hero is that piece of cardboard. And I'll give you the details on that. I'll tell you how thick it should be. I'll tell you where you can get it even. That's how much I want you to succeed. So here's what you'll need. First of all, you need a sales letter. And I'll be totally honest with you. The cardboard hero and the envelope, you know, a pile, B pile idea only goes so far. If your sales letter is awful, well, your results will not be so good. So you need a good sales letter. Second, you need an envelope. You can use a 9 by 12 envelope. That's what I would suggest. I would not certainly put this in a regular business size envelope. This needs to be a bigger envelope. 9 by 12 works just fine. You also need stamps. Now, if you've studied direct mail, you know that the fancier the stamp, the better. The more different the stamp, the better. The more unusual the stamp, the better. Not only that. Two stamps are better than one. Three stamps will pull better than two stamps or one stamps. Four stamps will pull even better and so on and so forth. So the stamps make a difference. Certainly the direct mail piece, the letter makes a difference. In addition, we've learned that if you write the words, do not bend on the envelope. Now, this looks like a sticker, but it's meant to be do not bend that you actually hand write. And the reason that's better than a sticker is because when you hand write it, it looks like it's personalized. And here is the rule of thumb. The more personalized the piece looks, the more likely you are to get it open. The more likely you are to get a response. So someone might say, wow, this sounds like a lot of work. Can I get a sticker? You can get a sticker, but it will make a slight difference in response. Just keep that in mind. And guys, please, I'm sorry, I can go on and on. If you want to jump in at any point, let me know, okay? No, you're doing great. I was going to get into the dimensions, but that makes sense. But if you make it too big to fit in a mailbox, that's perfect. Yes, exactly. You're going to talk about thickness, I guess. Yes. But you have to write do not bend. And I'm going to show you an example in a second. In addition, we found that if you put a photo or something that justifies the cardboard liner on the inside, it improves response. If it's just an envelope, a letter, and a stamp with a piece of cardboard in there, the recipient says, I don't get this. Why are they putting a piece of cardboard? And it may actually upset or anger some people like you're trying to pull one over on them. You have to be really careful of that. And so you actually put some photo or something that will justify having a piece of cardboard. Now, what you see on the bottom there, this is, I'll give you another little secret here. This I attached to a direct mail piece. And what this is, you might say, what is this guy? This is Alexander Graham Bell's first state to state phone call. This is an actual picture of it. And what I did was I attached the photo to a direct mail piece, to a direct mail letter. And in classic Gary Halbert fashion, I thought, I don't always use, as you can see, I've attached, you know, blah, blah, blah, there are two reasons for this. I don't always use this opening, but I thought this was perfect for this. And that's exactly what I did. And I'll read to you what I wrote and you can use it. You can adopt it to your needs. So what I said was, as you can see, I have attached a rare old photo of Alexander Graham Bell, the inventor of the telephone, because nowadays some people don't even know that, to the top of this letter. Why have I done this? Actually, there are two reasons. Number one, first, I have something extremely important to tell you, and I needed some way to make sure this letter would catch your attention. And two, and secondly, since this letter concerns the first telephone call, you should make regarding your blank, fill in the blank, regarding your, regarding your dentures, regarding your car accident, regarding your back injury, regarding your plumbing situation. I don't care what it is, fill in the blank. The whole idea is you're showing them what the first call should be. So the first call you should make regarding your car accident, I figured using this photo, showing Bell making his first phone call was especially appropriate. Here's what it's all about. And as Gary would say, if you can't take it from here and close the deal, what would he say? You're helpless. Yeah, if you're hopeless. So, but this, this is what cardboard hero, you know, it's not just about having a piece of cardboard that makes people, that makes the, the, the postal worker not bend it. And actually, let me get to it now. Now, these are called corrugated inserts. Where's my sheet here? Corrigated inserts. And this is one eighth of an inch thick. If you use anything less than an eighth of an inch thick, it can be bended without really breaking. They can bend it, the cardboard will bend. And if you have a tight mailbox, then they can just put it in there. So you have to use an eighth of an inch thick corrugated cardboard insert. So as you can see, this is not just about putting a piece of cardboard in an envelope. There's a lot more to it. But it's so simple that really, like I said, initially, the five year old can do it. You can get these at any office supply website. We personally use uline.com, u-l-i-n-e.com. They're, they're giants. They're, they're huge. They, they have every office shipping supply, you know, anything you need from there. So it's an eighth of an inch thick. And so, yes, you want it to be somewhat oversized. You can use the nine by 12 envelope. You can use even a little bigger size. And now it do even better, the 10 by 14, but that means it will cost more. The insert will be more expensive. And so, you know, the costs, relatively speaking, will increase as well. But I'm telling you under 250, you get the FedEx effect and blow everyone else away. That's a pretty, pretty sweet deal. Now, here's something we discovered that we didn't just put do not bend. We added the words photo enclosed. And by doing that, just those two words, it increased our response and additional 8%. Now, I'm not saying the response rate was 8%. I'm saying of those responding, it boosted it 8%. So if you were getting an eight, a 5% response rate, it went to 5.4, which as you know, in direct mail, especially if you are mailing in quantity, and especially if you're dealing with high priced items, it's huge. So this is a sample of what an envelope would look like. Now, please excuse the vertical line there. We actually needed to fold it to get it into the scanner. And so this was kind of last minute there. But you see the recipient, it's all handwritten. Now, the point is, like I said, the more you can handwrite and make it seem personal, as you know, the better it is. We have photo enclosed on one side, do not bend on the other. We actually, we use the reply address in the corner card. And the reason is a lot of what we do requires that. So we work with a lot of attorneys, and in fact, they have to put their name, not just the address, but the name also and the address. And so that's what we do. That's why we do it. And a lot of our clients... Do you have to identify that they're an attorney or you just put their name? Well, you have to put that they are an attorney. Industry standards, I mean, depending on what you're marketing, because that makes a difference, too. If you're selling Ferraris, you know, and you send a thousand letters, if you get one response and it closes the deal, it's good. But in general, if we're going to generalize, if you get, you know, a 1-2% response, that's incredible. Yeah. And that's how it's these attorneys... And Kevin, right? Yeah, you can make fortunes are made on 1-2% response, but on your case, that's those 30 pieces that arrived with your piece. You're averaging a response rate on those guys at 1%, 2%. Those guys, many of them didn't get anything. But if you average it all, that's just way below 9.5%. It's below 1%. It's way below... Even less than 1%. Do you want to hear the next one? Nah. Nah. We're good. Nah, we're done. Yeah, we got to go. Oh, sure. Why not? Twist our arm. Why not? Okay. Well, the other one, I will warn you in advance. It's a very poor performer, very, very poor. We've gotten up to an 8% response on that one. So you might not want to listen to it, but what the heck? Here we go. So the next one... I'm game out, stick around, it'll just be you and I. I knew you. I knew you. The article trick. And let me be honest with you, I used to... I never called this the article trick. I used to get... It was a horrible name. I called it the copy crusader, which frankly is awful. And the reason I am now calling it the article trick is thanks to Kevin Halbert. Because when I sent him the email, and I'm talking about copy crusader, and here's what I'm getting at. And he sent back a one line email, like, oh, the article trick. I'm like, oh, you know what, Kevin? That makes a lot of sense. It's called the article trick. And here's the idea. As you know, again, I work... Maybe I should elaborate a little bit more about this. You know I work with a lot of attorneys, but I also work with dentists. I also work with chiropractors. I also work with financial advisors and CPAs. A lot of professionals, professionals who have a lot of competition. And as you know, there's a lot of lead generation going on there. And so I had tested out, I said, I remember Gary writing something about the best way to get publicity or to get people interested, or to frankly get people's attention and make a sale is to have someone write this glowing review about you that makes it seem like it was written by a professional media person. And I thought, okay, this is a good idea. In addition to which the tear sheet mailers were really hot for a while, and I still see them. In fact, I got one about a month ago for a seminar. And so I thought if I can find a way, and those of you that don't know what a tear sheet mailer is, it is someone who's, let's say they're selling a seminar, and they make it look like someone, a reporter in a newspaper, actually wrote an article, a big article about this seminar presenter, and is giving us some details about an upcoming seminar, and you have to act quickly because all these people are interested. Basically, it's a great mail piece about a product. And the key about this is the piece looks like it's torn out of a newspaper. It actually has jagged edges, and it's folded up, and it's put in an envelope and mailed. And so this is the tear sheet mailer. And so I thought, well, I can find a way to perhaps combine the two. And so what I did, ended up doing was I had a professional journalist, actually someone who wrote for a living, write an article about me, as if she was very skeptical about what she's heard about me. And she was wondering, is this guy for real? Is it true what all these lawyers have said about him? Is he, in fact, someone who can help lawyers double, triple, quadruple their revenues in less than a year? Is it this, is it that? And basically raising all these questions and wondering, is this guy for real? And in the article, she ended up using the testimonials, and this lawyer said this, and that lawyer said that, and this lawyer said that. And then she said, and based on my research, and based on attending one of his seminars, and based on this, I can tell you that everything is absolutely true. So it comes from the perspective of skepticism. And that's a big difference in a lot of the mail pieces, where it's just a standard of, oh, sing the praises, this person is amazing, here's a great article about Joe Blow. No, it came from the perspective of, is he really for real? And that's really what the article trick is about. And so I had this journalist create the piece. I put a call to action at the end, because frankly, she was not business minded. She just wrote the piece like an investigative journalist. And I put a call to action, and we mailed it out, and I'll tell you, I'll give you a little more details about the piece and the mailing in a second. But that's the background of the piece. And oh, well, I forgot this part. And so why it's called the article trick, in that once it's written, it's formatted to look like an actual magazine article. And once you have it formatted that way, you insert it in a real magazine, and you make a photocopy with the magazine in the background and on the edges. To make it look like when the copy comes out, it has all black marks on it. It looks like it's in a magazine, it's slightly tilted. So it looks like it was in a magazine and was photocopied and was sent to you. There are a few more tricks I'll talk about in a second. Have I gotten your attention? Yeah, what I like is that it probably skewed a little bit, so you saw that all the pages like the thickness of the magazine. That's key. That's key because otherwise, if it's just a nice glossy piece, well, then it looks like an ad. The whole point of it is to look like it was in a magazine. Someone cared enough about you to make a copy of it out of their magazine and send it to you. That's the can. I'll tell you, there's a post-it and there are a few other steps to it. So that's the magazine trick. So thank you, Kevin Halbert, for coming up with the name. The reason why this method is so successful is because it uses social proof persuasion and then kicks it up a notch or two. And the social proof persuasion is someone who gets it and then makes a copy of it and then sends it to you. And I've even worked with some lawyers to make them... And I told them, make it look like it was actually copied more than once. So it was copied by one person and then they wrote a few notes on the sidebar, the empty area there in pencil with a very distinctive handwriting. And then it was copied again with a different person's handwriting with a completely different comment. And so this... So it's viral social proof. Viral social proof offline. So here's what you'll need for this trick. So here's what I created. I sometimes get in my obsessions and when I'm working, I want to create something on my own. I mean, I can give this to a professional designer, but I actually created the look of this article. I created it myself using Microsoft Word. I don't know how to use any of the other stuff. So I created this completely on my own. So let me just say that. This is the first page of the piece, the dirty little secret that makes lawyers rich. And so that's supposed to be the article. Now, it sort of looks like an article, but let's be honest. This is not entrepreneur magazine quality look. It just isn't. And frankly, I didn't care. I was so excited about the idea. I thought, let me test it out. And so in a second, I'll show you the three pages of the article, but you'll need an article. That's number one. Two, and hopefully you can see it on the bottom there. You'll need a post-it note. And of course, if you're able to personalize the post-it note with the recipient's first name, that's much better. And it says, Bill, this guy's the real deal. Exclamation point. And of course, you don't sign it with someone's actual name because the recipient of the piece might say, well, I don't know anyone named by that, tears it up and throws it out. So you have to use an initial. And the best initial to use is J because there are more J's in America than there are anything else. T, S, R, J is number one. So J gives you the highest probability of them receiving it and then go, oh, my friend, you know, Joe. Oh, thanks. Oh, wow, Joe. Thanks for remembering, you know, thinking of me. And so you use J. You also, like I said, you take that piece once you create it and you stick it in the middle of the spine there of the magazine so that when you make a copy, you know, the edges appear like they have pages on them. And as you know, when you have a magazine and you photocopy it, there are areas that are not flat on the copier. And so that, that ridgy or curved area turns black when you copy it and so forth and so on. The point is you put it in there and make a copy while it's in there to give it that authentic look when it gets copied. This doesn't really, I mean, this is an envelope, a plain envelope. Maybe you can't really tell from there because it's white, but it's a plain business size. Number 10 envelope, that's all it is. Also, stamp. And what we said before about the stamps applies here as well. If you use a unique stamp, that will, that will get more attention. The beauty of this, it's only going to cost you a first class, one first class stamp. That's it. That's all it is. And it's a small envelope. The costs on this are so small. Once you, there's the initial outlay, if you actually decide to hire someone to write an article, listen, you might want to do it yourself. And that's fine as well. You will save, all you're spending is the money for an envelope, a stamp and making copies. So it ends up being maybe, you know, 60 cents, 65 cents, if that. And a pencil. Now, why do you need a pencil? I alluded to this earlier. You need a pencil because once you make a copy, once you have the article and it's formatted before you make a copy, or even after. And remember, I told you, you might want to make it look like it was copied several times. You want to, you want to make like a little squiggly line, a few here and there. You want to, and I've done this, actually I've gotten quite good at it to make it look like it's little dust particles, you know, so you use the sort of the side of the pencil, the edge a little here and there to make it look like it was copied. And also to write a note here or there, you might underline something or do an arrow. And I've done this before, an arrow and the call to action or a great, you know, a great quote. And so this, these are the three pages of my article. Bill, this guy's the real deal. You see it. It's, it's in the middle. I didn't want to block the headline or the, or the subhead. I didn't want to block the picture because look at this handsome guy who wants to block that face. And, well, some people do actually. That's me, by the way. And it's in the middle. So they, of course, they see it. On the bottom, I made up a name of a magazine. And I made sure no one had this marketing success, you know, volume, eight, issue three, whatever it is, and there was a date there. And that's page one, page two. Now look, I mean, you see that arrow in the upper left. No real magazine is going to have an arrow, but you know what I said, I want to lead them into page two. So I said, why not? So it, it kind of looks like a magazine, but it doesn't really look like an authentic magazine publication. And I thought, to be honest with you, it wouldn't pull so well because it really didn't look like an actual magazine. You see the black areas, you know, you see on the sides, it looks like it was copied. And in subsequent, with clients and in subsequent versions, I mean, we had it look even more like a copy, a lot more black areas. And it looked really like it was, it had been copied several times. On page three, I purposely wanted to leave some open area so I can make, you know, write a couple of things. So I wrote like where, when at the end, when I said they can get my free report. This, by the way, just so that you know, supposedly the author of the article said, and she was at the seminar. And this guy, you know, Nate Aranis was talking and everyone was enthralled. And finally, I believe that what he's, you know, people have said about him is true. And at the end, he did something I couldn't believe he would do. He was actually giving away this, this free coveted report about, you know, how to get clients and droves and blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. And all of a sudden, as soon as he announced that I saw everyone's hand go up, they wanted a free copy. And I actually have gotten him to offer this free copy to you. And that's basically the call to action. And he allowed me to give out the number. You know, I mean, no real magazine is going to do this. And that's why, and I'll be honest with you, I didn't think this would do very well. And like I said, this was homemade. I mean, I made everything here. And so after doing it and after ma, after mailing it, again, it was a size 10 business envelope, a handwritten recipient, a stamp, and that's it. That's it. No name and the corner card, no name, no address. Okay. So let me, so do you want me to go on or does anyone have any questions here? I'm good. Okay. Let me tell you the steps in preparing this piece, just a few things to keep in mind. Number one, get a raving or investigatory, like I said, article written about you. And it's really a disguise sales pitch. But at the end, it's a call to action. You absolutely have to have the call to action. Otherwise, what's the point of even sending it out? Two, lay it out to look like a magazine style article. Now, I showed you that even a homemade layout that's somewhat kind of sort of resemblance in magazine style article will work. And I'll tell you how well in a second. Three, use the pencil to make notes, add little squiggly lines. Like if, you know, you make copies sometime, nowadays, the quality of copies are really good. But I'm talking about if you made copies like in the 80s, that kind of quality copier. Okay. You know, the little squiggly is in the little dust marks. And that's what you want. You want it to look authentically copied and inserted in a real magazine and make a photocopy of it on an angle or basically don't make it look perfect. The whole point in doing this is to make it look like it's genuine. And give it a real photocopy look. Now, like I said, use it, use the classic Gary Halbert curiosity inducing number 10 business envelope. Recipients name by hand, name and address by hand, a live stamp. You'll love this. You got it. You got to love, well, I won't say which group of professionals, but you can probably guess. You got to love this group. After doing all this and telling them, you know, all about how important personalizing it is and doing the article and doing the posted and doing the handwriting, the recipient's name. What do they do? They use a meter. They use a meter for the stamp. So totally kills the idea of it being a personalized, you know, hey, I got out of my book of stamps in my desk drawer and I'm sending it to you. Live stamp handwriting on the envelope should match the handwriting on the posted note. That's really important. And a lot of times people will give give the envelopes to other people to write the addresses and they'll pay them X number of dollars, you know, per envelope. And then they'll give the post it notes to somebody else. And the problem is you don't have consistency. You want to have the same handwriting, the same ink, the same pen, the same everything on the post it note that you do on the envelope. Do not write the sender's name or return address in the corner card. The article trick has worked incredibly well as a lead generating piece, generating a response rate as high as 8% to a cold list. So this isn't even your own list, your house list. This is a cold list. Some clients have taken the added step of giving it the exact magazine look where it really looks like it came out of a magazine. Prior to photocopying and they achieved a comparable. Some of them actually even achieved a slightly higher response rate than 8%. Not by much at all. It was frankly fairly negligible. But it's pretty incredible to think that you can do this all yourself in less than a week. Put the article together. You know, get some names, stuff them in an envelope. Well, put the post it note on the article, stuff it in an envelope, put some stamps on, send it out. And in less than a week, you could have real, genuine leads coming to you for, like I said, 60-ish cents per piece. So this is, in a nutshell, the article trick. And Nader, just a question on the post it note. I see you've used the black ink. Does it make a difference? Say it again? On the post it note, you've used black ink. Does it make a difference if it's blue or black? Actually, blue is better. I think it's supposed to be blue. Maybe it didn't come out really well in here, but blue is the best color. And if you're doing a direct mail letter, you always sign in blue and they call it reflex blue. And Ted Nicholas is a huge proponent of that and I've gotten some really good results with blue as well. So always blue. You know what I think for me would make it a little more genuine effect if you're really trying to pass it off as an article? Is that each magazine has their own weird little logo, like a dot that goes at the end of their article. Have you ever noticed that? Yes. Weird square. Sometimes it's just a square dot. When you go to print something that says home, blah, blah, blah, blah, that would make it real. But you're doing great. I mean, this must be great that this is just crushing it. I did a tear sheet mailing that was supposed to look like an article not too long ago. One of the things that I did to make it kind of look more like an article is two things. One, I like the main text and just use kerning. That's the one thing that looks very, you know, just looking at Microsoft Word under health. All it does is take the letters and move them closer together the way that professional type do. I mean, the main difference between when you look at something and say that was professionally done or not is just the kerning. I mean, they could choose any font or whatever. The other thing I did was I would start the very first letter of the article, like let's say it was an A or an I, I'd make it really big and bold in a different letter because I noticed that's what articles do. And what I did when I was doing this is I went and bought a bunch of newspapers and magazines and brought them home and started flipping through with the mixing and matching format stuff that I liked. Is direct mail your main form of marketing for your business? I mean, do you have online stuff as well? Or is it like 80% direct mail? How does it work? I wouldn't I wouldn't say 80%. I would say it's probably less than 50 at this point, only because. Frankly, I've been using referral marketing a lot. And so I've been using direct mail a little less, not because it's not working, but because frankly, you know, I'm able to get the clients without having to spend any money. But I would say it's about 40%. Direct mail is about 40%. And the second question was, for people who are just starting or who have never done direct mail, what tips or what advice would you give somebody who's just starting? Consume the Gary Halbert letter. How about that? That's I guess that's a given. But I mean it, though. I mean it because if you really are starting out, there is no better education you'll get. A few other things. Bypass conventional wisdom. And don't always go by what, you know, everyone is telling you. The people around you, especially who are maybe not marketers or don't do direct mail, they may be giving you bad advice. Also master headlines. Headlines are the lifeline of ads and the lifeline of direct mail letters. The better you get at headlines, the easier the rest of the piece will be. And certainly one more thing is always have and you know, we markers, we call it a hook or a big idea, but it really is important. Don't just come at it from, I want to just write a letter and offer my goods or services. There has to be a lot more to it than that. You have to have some idea, some hook, some concept that creates excitement that gets people interested. More than just I have a widget, would you like to buy it? Well, you know, what does this widget do? Why is it so special? What's the what's the story behind the widget? How was it created? Storytelling. This leads me, you know, I mean, you know, I can talk all night about this, but storytelling also. Very, very important. Get good at telling stories and that will take you, you know, along. It was all just taped to it and it had the stamps on the beach ball. I mean, it was this big. And the great thing was, it was one of those things, you know, it sits around the house. You know, the kids want to play with it. You don't want to throw it away. I mean, I was thinking about Ted Nicholas every single day for a long time because he said this beach ball, which reminds me of the coconut and the other things. It's about being, being different. And, you know, so the other aspect is, you know, pay attention to what you see. I had a client use, and Gary was brilliant at this in that he could put, I remember, one of his seminars where he said, give me anything, tell me anything, and I'll attach it to a letter and I'll tell you, you know, how I can create an introductory paragraph for it. And they used a couple of those shipping peanuts. You know? Oh, okay. And then, you know, basically, or, and then they used actual peanuts. I mean, and then, you know, you'd be nuts if you didn't take us up on this offer. I mean, you know, all kinds of different, different ways to work that in there. You know, the point is pretty much anything that you use, if it's 3D, if it stands out, if it's bulky, will almost always be better than not using it, assuming that it's cost effective. Yeah. I like foreign currency. I like foreign currency. And the reason I like that is because, well, one, you can get a lot of it cheap. My dad, I, you know, I found a bunch of Iraqi dinars and I used that one day and I slapped it on there and I go, you know, you see this Iraqi dinar? It's worthless because of the decisions made by the guy whose face is on it. It says worthless is the paper it's printed on. Do you feel like the decisions are, you know, being good for you? We're going to send this to this very pessimistic audience, right? That's awesome. One of the guys I gave it to, he's a provisional copyright, he's got it framed on his wall. Someone, you know, the original copy that they did. But the one thing I found that I thought was really interesting is people don't throw away foreign money. It doesn't make a difference if it's a cent or what it's worth. It's so much harder for them to actually take the money and throw it into the garbage. So everybody, a lot of households have this change of like worthless pieces of like, not just Canadian, but like, you know, I'm never going back to Costa Rica. And I've got just $3 and stuff like that. And I just heard recently that there was this, you know, place that, you know, I think it was in Bobway or whatever, that just went and they printed like the first trillion dollar bill. And if it was, if it was profitable enough, wouldn't it be great to send them a stack of cash, you know, a stack of foreign cash? You know, that's all, you know, a small stack and make that male lumpy. But and my dad, and I'm not the originator of that. I remember, Kevin, you remember having all those yen that dad had in his apartment? He had bags and bags of yen back when yen was like represented of a set less than a cent. I mean, far less than the cent. Wow. Just huge bags. It was such an interesting coin. And he did Wittos mites, which had a great story because the Wittos mites were back in the time of Jesus Christ. And I guess you can get them for cheap, even though, you know, they're 2000 years old. And then, you know, there's there's a whole story you could wrap around that whole sucker. Yeah, you couldn't throw that away. We are in and my dad loved the pitches by Clayton Maypeace, where they were selling like you know, 1800 silver dollars and I'm talking about how, you know, wider for somebody else might have been, you know, betting this kind of money on the tables and just putting those those kinds of stories to the thing. But often a crazy thing. Can can you tell us all about how the world can find you and. Sure. Yeah, it'd be my pleasure. Yeah. I actually have a newsletter, a free newsletter. Anyone who would like to sign up would be great. And I cover all kinds of different things. You know, I I didn't get to discuss PR. Excuse me. You can if you want. But if you want to leave it for that second step, that's fine, too. I didn't. Well, you tell me. I mean, I can I can tell you what I've done with PR. It's a cool story. What's the audience say? I feel like we love we love good stories. Yeah, what's that game show where everybody's in the squares? OK, I'm up for it. Yep. OK, so. You know, as you know, my my main target was attorneys. And I thought I have to I have to create this. I have to create a really crazy unique way to reach them. And I want to do it through PR. I had to remember reading as a kid. I classified that I still remember it to this day. Someone who and he wrote. I get millions of dollars in free advertising. Ask me how, you know, I never did ask him, but I still remember the ad. And I always thought I'd love to get free advertising. So. What I ended up doing was I thought the best way to get credibility with these attorneys, obviously, I'm an attorney myself, so that gives me advantage. But what I want to do is I want to form a nonprofit association. And being that lawyers have pretty much worse reputations and public opinion than even politicians. I said, let me go all out and really get people's attention. And so I created in the year 2000, I created a nonprofit. It's a 501 C3 called the American Lawyers Public Image Association. And the idea behind it was to create a positive public image of lawyers. And, you know, talk about an uphill battle, right? And so it just so happened at the time, I mean, it's, you know, when, when, when God is smiling on you, at the time there was a new show coming out about lawyers. And it was a surprise, surprise, portraying them as bottom feeders. And so I thought, okay, well, I started a new association and it's about the public images of lawyers. So, hey, I got to take a stand against this new show on NBC. The show is called First Years. So what I did was I put together one page press release. It's all it was, one page. And I wrote essentially that the American Lawyers Public Image Association is highly offended by the new show first year. It's offensive to lawyers. I mean, can you imagine anything being offensive to lawyers, right? And so, and I called for that, you know, I wanted a disclaimer before the show. How, you know, how dare you talk about lawyers that way, disclaimer or take the show off the air, we're taking a stand. This is unacceptable. Well, this was on a Tuesday. On a Thursday, I still remember this. I was sitting in my family room with my wife watching television. And, you know, we're sitting, we're hanging out. This was back in must-see TV when Friends was still on, on Thursday nights. And Friends goes to a commercial and all of a sudden the screen goes black. And I see a voiceover and words scrolling up the screen and it says, it has come to our attention that the American Lawyers Public Image Association are offended by our new show first year. And then they showed, you know, some clips of the show first year, first years, not first year, first year, some clips. And then at the end, there was music, loud music. And then the end, it just stopped suddenly and it said, we think that's the reason to watch. Joey first year, I was a debut Thursday night, blah, blah, blah. Let me tell you something. They ended up airing this promo. It was a 20 second promo. I have it on video, guys. I can, anyone who, who goes to jugularmarketing.com, you sign up for my newsletter. I'll send it to you and Bond and Mark and Kevin, I'll send it to you guys too. They ran that promo six times between then and when the show debuted. I think it was going to debut on a Monday night. Now, had I bought that advertising, it would have cost me for those 20, for the 20 seconds and must see TV, it would have cost me back then about 200 grand. So that was $1.2 million in free advertising, but that's not all that happened. I mean, as a result, the publicity that I got from this and the reporters who called me and the interviews that I gave and, and here, here's the kicker. You think all this is, is a who, here's the kicker. Well, Mr. Andes, obviously you take, you know, the public image association very seriously. And the image of lawyers very seriously. Can you tell us how many members you have? I knew that. It was just me. It was just me. You know, and so, you know, I kind of squirm my way out of it and oh, well, you know, it's we're a fairly new association and we, you know, we want to focus on the task at hand. We want to stop all this lawyer bashing, you know, it's like typical politician stuff, you know, and. Don't try to sidetrack me. Exactly. With the facts, right? And so, as a result, I mean, I got featured everywhere and, and because, and I parlayed and of course I got a lot of publicity with lawyers, but I parlayed this publicity to something else. And I promise I'll end the story in a minute or two. I parlayed this in creating the first ever National Love Your Lawyer Day. And on National Love Your Lawyer Day, number one, all lawyer jokes are banned. So, so you can't say anything bad about lawyers on that day. Number two, but there actually was a good part of it and that we encourage lawyers to do some pro bono work on that day and to give back. We also said for clients, if you, you know, have a lawyer in your family or you love a lawyer or you love your lawyer, who is actually your lawyer who does good work, you know, take care of them, take him out to lunch. This was 15 years ago. I am happy to report and I will let you in on a little secret here that I have not told anyone except probably my sons who are like my little marketing buddies. It reminds me of you guys, Kevin and Bond with your dad, you know. National Love My Lawyer Day is coming up in November. It's the first November, first Friday of every November, every year. And I have some really interesting things that I will be doing and I would suggest watch news anchors who are also lawyers because they may, shall I say, they may be mentioning National Love My Lawyer Day. I'll leave it at that. All right. But the publicity out of it literally. I mean, all of this stuff has been millions of dollars. If I were to purchase that stuff, oh, I forgot to tell you, I was on Access Hollywood. They featured, you know, the press release and I was on all over the place. With that free PR, how are you able to convert that to money? Were you just, maybe I've spaced out on something, but with all the free. It wasn't an actual conversion, but it was, it was the, the publicity that I received in being able to present myself to lawyers as someone who's been able to be here and be there and I've been featured nationally. And that's why I can command $3,000 an hour for lawyers because I have had all this national publicity. So it didn't have a direct impact at the time, but I knew I could parlay it in my fees. And you still could. What you could do is actually, you know, well, I don't know if I still put it back in the day, what you would do is put that on a TV and then film the TV. So they know that it was actually on the TV. And then, you know, because that's basically the same thing you're doing with the tear sheet, except you don't have to make it up. You can, you know, you clip that out for proof. But yeah, at least, you know, the branding for that's huge. Oh, that's, listen, like I said, I expect that there will be some interesting things. Keep your eyes peeled for early November and remember this conversation. Okay, lawyers lives matter too. I'm really glad you decided to stay and share that because that is one of the things that comes up repeatedly, regardless of if you're doing direct mail marketing or I am wherever you are, creating an association or some sort of professional organization to allow you. I mean, it's instant credibility. And if you're lucky enough to hit a home run like we did, it is killer social proof. So, you know, just for everybody who's new to all of this and may not realize when Kevin asked that question, how did you parlay it? You know, when you're looking at the bullet points of the different things that you need to do to create a credible sales letter or marketing piece, you know, those things, it's instantaneous. You know, those are no brainers for him now because he's just got it. So, you know, if you can come up with something like that, yeah. Christine, that's a great point. I actually call this marketing by association. But in terms of the question, I don't remember if it was Bond or Kevin that asked it and also my speaking fees shot up or at least I was demanding a lot more and getting it because obviously this president of this association who was on national television addressed by NBC media, it had a lot of people very curious and wanted to hear more. So, I would say it had a direct and an indirect impact. I think knowing the story behind it is more, raises your rate higher than the story in front of it. You know what I'm saying? Yeah, I think so. We were able to play them like that, yeah. Did you get out everything about how to get in contact with you? Like marketer? One of the things I'm doing is polling all of our speakers aside from the fantastic reading that is the Gary Halbert letters. Yes. What other books or things that you have read have impacted your marketing? What are some of the tomes that are like, oh, this is like the Bible? I've read all the classics, you know, Collier and Schwab, Ogilvy. I'll tell you Joe Sugarman's stuff. He has three or four great books that really are wonderful. Joe Sugarman triggers, I think is an absolute must read for anybody in sales or marketing should absolutely be required. I mean, I'm more of a classic book guy and I'll read them over and over as opposed to just look for the newest, hottest thing. Unless it's really recommended by someone I really trust and I think highly of their opinion because frankly, I don't have time to breathe. So, the old standbys I know are great and I just, you know, will reread them. Yeah, Joyce said the same thing. He said, if I could go back, I would have read the classics over and over again rather than reading more books. I would have read the other ones more repeatedly. Amazing. So much wisdom in that.
